topic: autonomous agents powered by large language models, tool-use, planning, reasoning, and multi-agent coordination
motivation: Multi-shot video generation demands high-level planning of shot sequences, character consistency, and narrative flow. Existing approaches rely on static storyboards or autoregressive generation, which often lack global coherence and flexibility. A rigorous theoretical model formalizing dynamic compositional planning as a graph optimization problem can provide a foundation for agentic systems, enabling efficient shot rearrangement, parallel generation, and constraint satisfaction while revealing fundamental computational limits.
hypothesis: Formalizing multi-shot planning as a graph optimization problem will reveal NP-hardness and yield efficient approximation algorithms that, when integrated into agentic systems, can improve coherence scores on benchmarks like MSVBench compared to sequential autoregressive methods.
method_sketch: We will conduct a theoretical analysis modeling multi-shot video generation as a dynamic graph: nodes represent shots with attributes (characters, actions, settings), edges represent feasible transitions based on continuity and narrative constraints. The planning problem is cast as finding an optimal path or subgraph maximizing a coherence metric under user constraints. We will (1) prove NP-hardness of the general problem, (2) identify tractable subcases (e.g., treewidth-bounded graphs), (3) design approximation algorithms with provable guarantees, (4) incorporate uncertainty via probabilistic edges, and (5) outline integration with diffusion-based generation as a constraint satisfaction process. The analysis will be purely theoretical, but we will illustrate algorithms with simple Python simulations on synthetic scenarios derived from MSVBench's annotation schema.
expected_outcome: A theoretical framework with complexity classification, approximation algorithms, and integration guidelines. Publish as conference paper (e.g., NeurIPS, ICLR). Provide open-source Python library implementing the algorithms for synthetic validation.
title: Graph-Theoretic Complexity and Approximations for Multi-Shot Video Planning

provenance.resources_text: Theoretical work requires no experimental data; synthetic scenarios are generated algorithmically. Compute: standard laptops for simulations. Software: Python for illustrative code. Team: 2-3 researchers with expertise in graph algorithms, computational complexity, and generative models.

topic: agents, distillation
motivation: Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents require extensive training time to converge to optimal policies, which is impractical for many real-world applications. This research aims to accelerate RL convergence by leveraging transductive distillation techniques, transferring knowledge from a teacher model to a student model, while ensuring robustness and generalization. The motivation is to provide a theoretically grounded approach to transductive distillation in RL, backed by mathematical analysis and proofs, and to demonstrate its effectiveness across a broad range of RL algorithms and environments.
hypothesis: Transductive distillation will lead to a significant reduction in the number of episodes required for the RL student model to match the performance of the teacher model, while maintaining or enhancing the final performance levels. Additionally, the student model will demonstrate robustness and generalization capabilities on a diverse set of unseen environments. Specifically, we hypothesize that distillation will reduce the number of training episodes required for convergence by at least 20%, and improve the final average reward by at least 10% compared to a non-distilled RL model.
method_sketch: This research will involve a two-pronged approach: theoretical analysis and empirical study. Theoretical analysis will develop a rigorous mathematical framework to explain why transductive distillation should improve RL convergence. This will involve deriving lower bounds on the learning rate and convergence time, and analyzing the conditions under which distillation leads to improved sample efficiency and robustness. Empirical study will apply transductive distillation to a diverse set of RL algorithms (DQN, A3C, PPO, SAC) on a variety of benchmark tasks (Atari games, Mujoco environments, robotic control tasks). We will extract intermediate representations from a trained teacher model and incorporate them into the student model's learning process to accelerate convergence. To prevent overfitting, we will employ regularization techniques such as dropout and weight decay, as well as adversarial training. We will also evaluate the student model's generalization capabilities on unseen environments.
metrics: sample efficiency, robustness, generalization performance, final average reward
expected_outcome: We aim to demonstrate at least a 20% reduction in the number of training episodes required for convergence and a 10% improvement in the final average reward compared to a non-distilled RL model. Furthermore, the student model will show comparable performance to the teacher model on a diverse set of unseen environments, indicating robustness and generalization. We will also provide theoretical insights into why distillation accelerates convergence and enhances robustness in RL.
title: Advancing Reinforcement Learning Convergence through Theoretically Grounded Transductive Distillation

provenance.resources_text: We will use benchmark RL datasets such as OpenAI Gym and create custom tasks if necessary. Compute resources will include access to GPU clusters. We will leverage existing RL software frameworks such as TensorFlow and PyTorch for implementation. Theoretical analysis will require mathematical tools and simulation environments to model the distillation process. We will also incorporate additional evaluation metrics such as sample efficiency, robustness, and generalization performance.

topic: graph neural networks for molecular property prediction, drug discovery, social network analysis, knowledge graph reasoning, and recommendation systems
motivation: Current methods for building equivariant neural networks either rely on explicit group representation theory (e.g., Tensor Field Networks) which becomes intractable for large symmetry groups like space groups, or use frame averaging which is general but can be computationally expensive and lacks theoretical guarantees on expressivity. There is a need to combine the strengths of both approaches to create a single architecture that is both expressive and efficient for any symmetry group, particularly for large-scale 3D systems in materials science and structural biology.
hypothesis: The hybrid framework will achieve comparable or better accuracy than pure frame averaging or TFNs on benchmark tasks (e.g., QM9, Materials Project) while significantly reducing computational cost (FLOPs and memory) for large groups. Moreover, it will enable exact equivariance for any space group without requiring explicit construction of irreducible representations.
method_sketch: We propose to develop a theoretical framework that unifies the frame averaging approach (as in the frontier paper) with tensor field networks (TFNs) by constructing adaptive frames that are learned via the neural network itself, while ensuring exact invariance/equivariance. Specifically, we will derive conditions under which the frame averaging operator can be approximated by a learned equivariant basis, reducing the need to average over many group elements. The core idea is to parameterize the space of frames as an equivariant embedding and then use a small set of representative frames that capture the essential symmetries. We will then analyze the expressivity of this hybrid approach, proving that it can approximate any continuous equivariant function on graphs with symmetry constraints, and provide bounds on sample complexity.
dataset: QM9, Materials Project, Protein Data Bank
expected_outcome: 1) A theoretical proof of universal approximation for the hybrid architecture under symmetry groups. 2) Implementation of the method in an open-source library. 3) Experimental results showing at least 20% reduction in inference time and memory usage compared to full frame averaging, while maintaining or improving accuracy on property prediction tasks. 4) A comprehensive analysis of the trade-offs between expressivity and efficiency across different symmetry groups.
title: A Unified Framework for Equivariant Graph Neural Networks via Frame Averaging and Tensor Field Networks: Bridging Expressivity and Efficiency

provenance.resources_text: Datasets: QM9 (molecules), Materials Project (crystals), Protein Data Bank (proteins). Compute: High-performance GPU cluster (e.g., NVIDIA A100). Software: PyTorch, e3nn, Equivariant Graph Neural Network libraries, custom code. Existing implementations of frame averaging and TFNs for baseline comparisons.

topic: graph transformers, heterogeneous graph learning, temporal graph networks, and scalable graph neural network architectures
motivation: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative learning across distributed IoT devices, but existing FL approaches for healthcare often rely on static graph models or simple neural networks that cannot capture the temporal dynamics and complex relationships in patient data. Meanwhile, dynamic graph transformers have shown promise in modeling disease progression from electronic health records, but they are computationally heavy and require centralized data, raising privacy concerns. Bridging these two streams—by designing a lightweight, resource-aware dynamic graph transformer that can operate in a federated setting—would allow real-time, privacy-preserving analysis of time-varying health data from wearable sensors and edge devices, enhancing early detection and personalized care while respecting resource constraints.
hypothesis: The proposed federated dynamic graph transformer will achieve comparable or better accuracy in predicting health events (e.g., arrhythmia, sepsis) compared to centralized baselines, while reducing communication cost by at least 30% and maintaining privacy. Additionally, it will demonstrate adaptability to varying resource constraints without significant performance degradation.
method_sketch: We propose a system design that integrates a dynamic graph transformer model with a split federated learning framework. The model consists of a lightweight encoder deployed on edge devices (e.g., wearables) that extracts temporal features from sensor data and constructs local dynamic graphs representing patient state transitions. These local graphs are aggregated at a central server using a novel subtree attention mechanism that preserves temporal structure while performing federated aggregation. The server updates a global dynamic graph transformer and sends back only necessary parameters to edge devices, minimizing communication overhead. We will incorporate adaptive resource awareness by dynamically adjusting model complexity based on device capabilities (CPU, memory, battery) and network conditions. The design will be validated through simulation using real-world health IoT datasets.
expected_outcome: A fully designed system architecture, prototype implementation, and evaluation results showing: (1) prediction accuracy (AUROC, F1) on benchmark tasks; (2) communication efficiency (bytes per round, total rounds); (3) resource usage profiles (CPU, memory, battery) on edge devices; (4) privacy analysis (no raw data leaving devices).
title: Federated Dynamic Graph Transformer for Real-Time Health Monitoring in Resource-Constrained IoT Edge Networks

provenance.resources_text: Datasets: MIMIC-III (ICU data), PhysioNet Challenge 2020 (sepsis), WESAD (stress detection from wearables). Compute: Cloud GPU instances for server simulation, edge device emulators (Raspberry Pi). Software: PyTorch, FedML, PyTorch Geometric Temporal, custom simulators.

topic: retrieval augmented generation and tool augmented language models
motivation: Multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like healthcare and security, but they face unique challenges: cross-modal alignment, stealth poisoning attacks via metadata, interpretability, and evaluation across modalities. Existing RAG evaluation surveys focus mainly on text, lacking standardized benchmarks for multimodal contexts and adversarial robustness. By bridging insights from multimodal RAG (Cluster A) and RAG evaluation (Cluster B), we can develop a comprehensive evaluation framework that not only measures performance but also tests resilience against poisoning and interpretability, thereby advancing trustworthy multimodal AI.
hypothesis: The framework will reveal that multimodal RAG systems using cross-modal dense retrieval with metadata sanitization and concept-enhanced generation achieve the best trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and interpretability, and that current benchmarks underestimate vulnerability to metadata poisoning.
method_sketch: Design a modular system that: (1) integrates multiple retrieval strategies (dense, sparse, cross-modal) and generation backbones (LLMs, VLMs) for flexible experimentation; (2) incorporates a benchmark suite with diverse multimodal tasks (e.g., radiology report generation, species recognition, spreadsheet QA) and includes metadata-driven poisoning attacks to simulate stealth threats; (3) defines evaluation metrics covering factuality, cross-modal alignment, interpretability (via concept attribution), and robustness; (4) provides an open-source toolkit to enable reproducible evaluation and comparison of multimodal RAG systems.
expected_outcome: A publicly released benchmark suite and evaluation toolkit for multimodal RAG, with baseline results and analysis. At least 3-5 state-of-the-art systems compared, identifying strengths/weaknesses. Demonstrated effectiveness of poisoning defenses and interpretability methods.
title: Multimodal RAG Evaluation Framework with Poison-Resistant Benchmarking

provenance.resources_text: Datasets: MIMIC-CXR (radiology), iNaturalist (species), WikiTables (spreadsheets). Compute: GPU cluster for training/evaluation. Software: PyTorch, Hugging Face Transformers, FAISS, custom evaluation scripts.

topic: Deep learning for real-time stress prediction in structural components
motivation: The prediction of structural stress is crucial for preventing infrastructure failure. Existing methods are not optimized for real-time high-dimensional data processing, leading to inefficiencies and inaccuracies. This research aims to develop a tool library that addresses these limitations by providing a scalable, efficient, and accurate solution for real-time stress prediction in large infrastructure systems.
hypothesis: The proposed CNN architecture with the multi-scale feature extraction module will outperform current methods in terms of prediction accuracy and real-time processing capabilities when evaluated on high-dimensional sensor data from structural components.
method_sketch: The method involves the creation of a deep learning model architecture optimized for real-time processing of high-dimensional sensor data. The proposed architecture will be a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a novel multi-scale feature extraction module designed to handle variable input sizes. This module will leverage compressed sensing techniques to reduce the dimensionality of the input data while preserving essential features for accurate stress prediction. The CNN will be fine-tuned using a combination of backpropagation and stochastic gradient descent. The tool library will be implemented in Python, utilizing TensorFlow for neural network development, and will include APIs for easy integration with existing sensor systems.
dataset: Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) benchmark, University of Bristol
metrics: prediction accuracy, real-time processing capabilities, latency
expected_outcome: A functional tool library with an API that can be used by researchers and engineers for real-time stress prediction in structural components. The library should demonstrate improved accuracy and reduced latency compared to existing methods, with benchmarks against current state-of-the-art models. The library will be validated on multiple datasets to ensure robustness and generalizability.
title: Developing a Scalable High-Dimensional Real-Time Stress Prediction Tool Library

provenance.resources_text: We will use the publicly available dataset from the Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) benchmark, specifically the one provided by the University of Bristol. The library will be implemented in Python, utilizing TensorFlow for neural network development. The required computational resources will include GPUs for training and testing the models.

topic: Strategic Extensions for IdeaGraph: Fostering Advanced Scientific Creativity and Intelligence
motivation: The advancement of scientific creativity and intelligence requires an intuitive interface to navigate the complex network of scientific ideas and facilitate interdisciplinary connections. IdeaGraph, a theoretical framework for ideation, lacks a user-friendly interface that enables researchers to input and visualize ideas, hindering its practical application and potential impact.
hypothesis: Researchers using the IdeaGraph interface will demonstrate statistically significant improvement in generating novel ideas and identifying interdisciplinary connections, compared to those using traditional literature review methods and existing creativity-enhancing tools.
method_sketch: This study will develop and evaluate a prototype of a user-friendly interface for IdeaGraph. The interface will utilize named entity recognition (NER) models for input parsing, specifically leveraging the BERT-based BioBERT model fine-tuned for scientific literature, and a graph visualization library (D3.js) for displaying connections. A controlled experiment will compare the creativity and interdisciplinary understanding of users who interact with the IdeaGraph interface against a control group that uses traditional literature review methods and existing creativity-enhancing tools. The evaluation will include both quantitative metrics (idea novelty, diversity of citations) and qualitative analysis (user interviews, think-aloud protocols).
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is a 20% improvement in idea generation efficiency and a 30% increase in interdisciplinary connection identification over the baseline traditional literature review method, measured by subjective assessment and idea novelty metrics such as the diversity of citations in the proposed ideas. Additionally, qualitative analysis will provide insights into the user experience and the creative process facilitated by the IdeaGraph interface.
title: IdeaGraph Interface: Enhancing Scientific Creativity through User-Friendly Visual Interaction

provenance.resources_text: The project will utilize existing datasets such as the Semantic Scholar Corpus for scientific concept extraction, and the D3.js library for graph visualization. Compute resources for developing and testing the interface, as well as software for statistical analysis (e.g., R, Python), will be required. No new datasets will be created, but existing ones will be leveraged effectively.

topic: agents, distillation
motivation: The challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) is to find policies that perform well across a range of complex environments. Knowledge distillation can potentially enhance the convergence rate and overall performance of RL agents by transferring knowledge from more complex models to simpler ones. However, there is a lack of systematic exploration of distillation strategies tailored for RL, often borrowing techniques from supervised learning without fully addressing the unique properties of RL tasks.
hypothesis: A student RL agent trained via distillation from a teacher network optimized by NAS will achieve a higher performance with faster convergence compared to standard distillation methods.
method_sketch: This research will construct an interdisciplinary bridge between reinforcement learning and neural architecture search (NAS) to develop a novel distillation framework. We hypothesize that by optimizing the network architecture for knowledge transfer in RL, we can significantly improve the efficiency and effectiveness of knowledge distillation. The method involves training a teacher network to solve a given RL task, identifying the most informative features through NAS, and distilling this knowledge into a more compact student network. The NAS component will be adapted to find architectures that are not only good at representation learning but also facilitate efficient knowledge transfer.
dataset: OpenAI Gym environment datasets (e.g., CartPole, MountainCar, etc.)
expected_outcome: The proposed approach will yield a 20% improvement in convergence speed and a 15% increase in final performance metrics (e.g., cumulative reward) of the student RL agent compared to baseline distillation methods.
title: Knowledge Transfer in Reinforcement Learning via Interdisciplinary Bridge to Neural Architecture Search

provenance.resources_text: {'datasets': 'OpenAI Gym environment datasets (e.g., CartPole, MountainCar, etc.) for RL tasks', 'compute': 'High-performance GPU clusters for training complex neural networks and NAS', 'software': 'TensorFlow/Keras for neural network implementation, custom-built NAS framework'}

topic: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for Stress Field Prediction in Irregular Structures
motivation: Stress field prediction in irregular structures is crucial for applications in structural engineering and materials science. Current Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) show promise for such tasks but are limited due to the complexity and irregularity of the structures. By combining insights from recurrent GNNs with advanced deep learning techniques such as Sequential Signal Mixing Aggregation, we aim to develop a novel tool library that significantly improves the accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability of stress field predictions.
hypothesis: StressPredict will outperform existing GNN-based stress prediction models in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability. The integration of PINNs and logical explanations will enable the tool to provide more reliable stress field predictions on irregular structures.
method_sketch: We will design and implement a tool library called StressPredict that integrates advanced GNN architectures with physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). The library will include novel algorithms for: 1) Role-aware rewiring and equitable partitioning of irregular structures to enhance the representation learning capabilities of GNNs. 2) Physics-informed loss functions that incorporate physical laws governing stress field propagation in materials. 3) A logical explanation framework for mean aggregation in GNNs to improve interpretability. We will provide clear API documentation, tutorials, and benchmark the tool against existing stress prediction models on publicly available datasets like the RAFFLES dataset.
expected_outcome: StressPredict will achieve at least 10% higher prediction accuracy on the RAFFLES dataset compared to state-of-the-art methods. The tool will reduce training time by 20% and provide interpretable explanations for stress field predictions.
title: StressPredict: A Tool Library for Enhancing Graph Neural Networks for Stress Field Prediction in Irregular Structures

provenance.resources_text: We will utilize the RAFFLES dataset for training and testing, require a multi-GPU server for training large GNN models, and leverage existing software libraries like PyTorch Geometric and TensorFlow.

topic: Autonomous Novelty Agents in Real-Time Chat
motivation: Conversational agents in real-time chat systems require dynamic adaptability to maintain user engagement and satisfaction. However, current models lack the ability to refine their responses based on real-time user feedback. This research aims to address this gap by developing an adaptive learning mechanism that leverages sentiment analysis and reinforcement learning to improve agent responses in real-time.
hypothesis: The adaptive learning mechanism will result in a statistically significant improvement in user satisfaction, measured by a composite metric of engagement duration, message frequency, and sentiment scores, and a reduction in user dissatisfaction, measured by negative sentiment scores and disengagement rates, compared to a static response model.
method_sketch: We will develop a feedback-driven reinforcement learning model using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), trained on a diverse dataset of Reddit and Twitter interactions. The model will learn a policy that maximizes a quantified measure of user satisfaction. Sentiment analysis tools from Hugging Face's transformers will be employed to process real-time user feedback and generate dynamic reward signals. The adaptive learning mechanism will incorporate a novel exploration-exploitation strategy, such as epsilon-greedy with annealing, to balance between trying new strategies and exploiting known successful ones. We will define user satisfaction as a composite metric based on engagement duration, message frequency, and sentiment scores, which will be integrated into the model's reward system.
metrics: engagement duration, message frequency, sentiment scores, negative sentiment scores, disengagement rates
expected_outcome: The research is expected to demonstrate a 20% improvement in the composite user satisfaction metric and a 15% reduction in user dissatisfaction rates compared to a baseline model without adaptive learning.
title: Enhancing Conversational Agent Adaptability through Real-Time Sentiment-Driven Learning

provenance.resources_text: The project will require: 1) Reddit and Twitter datasets for training and validation; 2) computing resources for training the PPO model; 3) Hugging Face's transformers for sentiment analysis; 4) TensorFlow or PyTorch for implementing the machine learning algorithms; 5) additional natural language processing tools for dialogue state tracking and user intent recognition.

topic: new way to develop novelty llm from scratch
motivation: Modern large language models are used in various applications, including software engineering and cybersecurity. In the realm of cybersecurity, identifying vulnerabilities and threats in web infrastructures can be accelerated by utilizing LLM-driven agentic systems. We hypothesize that combining the 3D Roofline Model for optimized LLM decompression with a multi-agent framework dedicated to domain-specialized log parsing could significantly enhance the performance and accuracy of threat identification in LLM-driven systems.
hypothesis: The 3D Roofline Model-enhanced multi-agent LLM framework will outperform the baseline LLM-driven system in threat identification speed and log parsing accuracy, due to better optimization of memory access and specialization of parsing tasks.
method_sketch: This empirical study will design and conduct rigorous experiments comparing the performance of a baseline LLM-driven system with a proposed 3D Roofline Model-enhanced multi-agent LLM framework on a standard log parsing dataset. The proposed system will leverage the 3D Roofline Model for optimizing data decompression and memory access patterns while employing log parsing agents specialized in various cybersecurity domains, such as web application security, user access anomalies, and system breaches. The framework will be evaluated against the Hugging Face Transformers' dataset for log parsing tasks, using metrics such as processing time, accuracy in parsing, and threat identification speed.
dataset: Hugging Face Transformers' dataset for log parsing tasks
metrics: processing time, accuracy in parsing, threat identification speed
expected_outcome: A measurable 20% improvement in threat identification speed and 15% increase in log parsing accuracy compared to the baseline LLM-driven system.
title: 3D Roofline Model-Enhanced Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Log Parsing

provenance.resources_text: The Hugging Face Transformers' dataset for log parsing tasks, cloud computing resources for parallelizing experiments, and software tools for implementing the 3D Roofline Model and multi-agent communication protocols.

topic: agents swarm and llms
motivation: AI code agents (vibe coding, autonomous coding assistants) are increasingly used in real software projects, yet no dataset systematically captures the security and safety failures that emerge specifically from their autonomous decision-making during development tasks—such as prompt injection via malicious code comments, privilege escalation through tool misuse, or unsafe code generation that bypasses human review. Existing security benchmarks (e.g., CyberSecEval, SecurityEval) focus on generated code vulnerabilities, not on agent-level autonomy-induced risks during the development workflow itself. Bridging Cluster A (AI-assisted dev) and Cluster B (agentic security risks) requires a dataset that captures both the development context and the safety failure mode.
hypothesis: A dataset annotated with agent_autonomy_level and development_phase will enable classifiers to achieve macro-F1 ≥ 0.70 on failure_taxonomy detection—at least 15 F1 points above models trained on existing code-vulnerability datasets (SecurityEval, CyberSecEval) applied to the same task, demonstrating that workflow-context annotations are necessary for agentic safety risk detection.
method_sketch: 1. COLLECTION: Mine 500+ real incidents from GitHub issues/PRs, StackOverflow, HackerNews postmortems, and CVE reports (2022–2025) where an AI coding assistant (Copilot, Cursor, Devin, Claude Code) contributed to a security or safety failure. Supplement with 300 synthetic scenarios generated by prompting GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet with adversarial developer contexts (e.g., malicious dependency suggestions, insecure scaffolding). 2. ANNOTATION SCHEMA: Each record annotated with (a) agent_autonomy_level: {supervised, semi-autonomous, fully_autonomous}, (b) failure_taxonomy: {prompt_injection, privilege_escalation, insecure_codegen, memory_poisoning, tool_misuse, human_override_bypass}, (c) development_phase: {planning, scaffolding, implementation, review, deployment}, (d) human_oversight_present: bool, (e) exploitability_score: CVSS-style 0–10, (f) recovery_action: free-text. 3. QUALITY: Three-annotator agreement with Cohen's Kappa target ≥ 0.75 on failure_taxonomy; automated consistency checks using a fine-tuned RoBERTa classifier trained on CWE descriptions to flag label mismatches. 4. DIVERSITY ANALYSIS: Measure coverage across all 6 failure taxonomy classes, 3 autonomy levels, and 4 development phases; ensure no single class exceeds 35% of records. 5. BASELINE BENCHMARKS: Evaluate GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Llama-3-70B on a held-out 20% split using F1 per failure_taxonomy class and macro-F1 as primary metrics, establishing detection baselines.
baselines: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3-70B, SecurityEval, CyberSecEval
expected_outcome: SafeDevBench: 800+ records, Cohen's Kappa ≥ 0.75, balanced across taxonomy classes (no class >35%), with published annotation guidelines, inter-annotator agreement report, diversity statistics, and baseline macro-F1 scores for 3 LLMs. Dataset released on HuggingFace with CC-BY-4.0 license.
title: SafeDevBench: A Annotated Dataset of Security-Safety Incidents in AI-Assisted Software Development Workflows

provenance.resources_text: Public GitHub API, StackOverflow data dump (archive.org), NVD CVE database; GPT-4o API and Claude 3.5 Sonnet API for synthetic generation (~$500 estimated); 3 annotators at ~40 hours each; RoBERTa-base fine-tuning on a single A100 GPU (~8 hours); HuggingFace Datasets for hosting; Label Studio for annotation UI.

topic: IdeaGraph: A Multi-Agent Framework for Graph-Structured, Execution-Aware Research Ideation with Quality-Diversity Optimization
motivation: The rapid growth of scientific knowledge across various domains necessitates the development of innovative methods to synthesize and leverage this information effectively. This research aims to enhance the ideation process by integrating diverse knowledge sources into a unified framework, fostering interdisciplinary collaboration and innovation.
hypothesis: The integration of diverse knowledge sources into the IdeaGraph framework, using a multi-agent system with NLP-based data assimilation and QDO techniques, will result in a measurable increase in the novelty and quality of research ideas generated, as compared to traditional methods.
method_sketch: We propose a concrete theoretical framework for a scalable, heterogeneous Knowledge Graph framework, named IdeaGraph, which integrates information from various scientific databases such as PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus. The framework will consist of a multi-agent system where each agent specializes in a specific knowledge source. Each agent will employ a combination of Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, such as Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Text Classification, to assimilate, merge, and consolidate information from its respective source. To handle data heterogeneity, we will develop a modular ontology mapping algorithm that aligns different knowledge representations. For scalability, we will implement distributed computing techniques and data partitioning strategies. The framework will utilize Quality-Diversity Optimization (QDO) techniques, specifically MAP-Elites, to generate a diverse set of high-quality research ideas.
expected_outcome: We aim to achieve at least a 20% improvement in the QD-Score, a composite metric combining novelty and quality, over the baseline system without knowledge integration. This will be measured through a comparative analysis of ideation outputs from both systems. Additionally, we will evaluate the scalability of the IdeaGraph framework by assessing its performance with increasing volumes of data.
title: Advancing Research Ideation through a Scalable, Heterogeneous Knowledge Graph Framework

provenance.resources_text: We will require access to scientific databases such as PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus. Computational resources will include high-performance servers for multi-agent system training and execution. Software tools will include NLTK and SpaCy for NLP tasks, NetworkX for graph processing, TensorFlow for machine learning tasks, and custom scripts for data assimilation and QDO implementation.

topic: large language model reasoning and planning with novelty agents
motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in natural language processing but struggle with symbolic reasoning. Integrating them with novelty agents, capable of abstract reasoning, can significantly improve their performance on complex reasoning tasks. This research aims to create a modular framework that seamlessly integrates novelty agents with LLMs to tackle reasoning tasks requiring both language understanding and symbolic reasoning.
hypothesis: The integration of LLMs with novelty agents using our modular framework will result in a significant improvement in accuracy and task completion times on complex reasoning tasks, compared to using LLMs alone.
method_sketch:
We will develop a tool library for integrating novelty agents with LLMs. The library will include:
1. APIs for bidirectional communication between novelty agents and LLMs.
2. Plugins for symbolic reasoning tasks, allowing the agents to perform operations like logical deduction and mathematical reasoning.
3. A framework for representing novel experiences, enabling the agents to learn from new situations.
The modular architecture will be designed to accommodate different novelty agents and reasoning tasks. Each agent will be defined by a clear specification outlining its capabilities and how it differs from traditional symbolic reasoning systems.
To test the framework, we will use a combination of existing datasets (MATH, AI2 Math, CommonsenseQA) and create new datasets focusing on complex reasoning tasks that benefit from the integration of LLMs and novelty agents. The datasets will be designed to cover a diverse range of tasks, including mathematical reasoning, logical deduction, and commonsense reasoning.
dataset: MATH dataset for mathematical reasoning, AI2 Math dataset, CommonsenseQA dataset for commonsense reasoning, Newly created datasets focusing on complex reasoning tasks
expected_outcome: A comprehensive tool library with documentation, benchmarks, and test cases demonstrating improved accuracy and reduced task completion times on complex reasoning tasks. The outcomes will be measured against a well-defined baseline, showing a minimum of 10% improvement in accuracy and reduced task completion times.
title: Enhancing Symbolic Reasoning in LLMs with a Modular Novelty Agent Framework

provenance.resources_text: {'datasets': ['MATH dataset for mathematical reasoning', 'AI2 Math dataset', 'CommonsenseQA dataset for commonsense reasoning', 'Newly created datasets focusing on complex reasoning tasks'], 'compute': 'Access to high-performance computing clusters for training and testing the integration', 'software': 'TensorFlow/PyTorch for building machine learning models, existing LLM frameworks for integration'}

topic: new way to develop novelty llm
motivation: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in natural language processing tasks; however, their ability to process multimodal inputs such as images, audio, and video alongside text is limited. This limitation restricts their application in fields requiring contextual understanding of diverse data types, such as autonomous vehicles and virtual assistants. The development of a scalable and flexible toolkit that enables LLMs to effectively integrate and process multimodal data is crucial for expanding their applicability and performance.
hypothesis: The integration of the Scalable Multimodal Fusion Toolkit into existing LLM architectures will lead to a statistically significant improvement in performance on multimodal tasks over baseline LLMs without such integration.
method_sketch:
We propose to develop a Scalable Multimodal Fusion Toolkit (SMFT) that can be integrated into existing LLM architectures to enhance their multimodal processing capabilities. The toolkit will consist of the following components:
1. **Novel Data Fusion Algorithms**: We will design and implement algorithms that can effectively fuse multimodal data streams, leveraging techniques such as cross-modal attention and transformer-based architectures. Specifically, we will introduce a novel algorithm called 'Multimodal Fusion Transformer' (MFT) that extends the transformer architecture to handle multimodal inputs by learning cross-modal attention weights.
2. **Attention Mechanisms for Multimodality**: We will develop attention mechanisms tailored for multimodal data, allowing the model to focus on relevant information from different modalities. Our proposed 'Cross-Modal Attention' (CMA) mechanism will enable the model to selectively attend to features from different modalities based on their relevance to the task.
3. **Optimization Strategies**: We will explore optimization techniques for training LLMs on multimodal datasets, including gradient-based methods and reinforcement learning. We will adapt these strategies to handle the unique challenges of multimodal data, such as synchronizing learning across modalities and handling different data types.
4. **Modular Architecture**: The toolkit will be designed as a set of modular APIs and modules that can be flexibly combined with different LLM architectures.
dataset: Conceptual Captions, Flickr30k Entities, Visual Genome
expected_outcome: We expect the SMFT to achieve at least a 10% improvement in accuracy on multimodal tasks such as image captioning, video description, and audio-text classification compared to baseline LLMs. Additionally, we anticipate faster convergence and better generalization to unseen multimodal data.
title: Developing a Scalable Multimodal Fusion Toolkit for Enhancing Language Models

provenance.resources_text: We will utilize publicly available multimodal datasets such as Conceptual Captions, Flickr30k Entities, and Visual Genome. The toolkit will be developed in Python, leveraging existing machine learning libraries like TensorFlow or PyTorch. topic: generative AI and agents
motivation: Production deployments of tool-using agents (e.g., OpenAI Function Calling, LangChain AgentExecutor, AutoGPT) grant API access through ad-hoc allow-lists with no formal worst-case safety guarantees. A single over-privileged tool call can exfiltrate data, trigger irreversible side-effects, or chain into privilege escalation. There is no principled framework that derives the *minimal* permission set sufficient for a task, nor bounds on the blast radius of a compromised or hallucinating agent. This gap blocks enterprise adoption.
hypothesis: MinSandbox will achieve ≥95% soundness (never under-grants) and ≥80% precision (within 1.25x of minimal grant size) on the 50 annotated ToolBench traces, outperforming the current practice of full-permission grants (precision ≈ 0.1 on the same traces) and manual allow-lists from two open-source agent frameworks (LangChain v0.2, AutoGPT v5).
method_sketch: We develop a formal trust calculus in three layers. (1) Permission Lattice: Model each external tool/API as a tuple (R, W, X, I) — read, write, execute, irreversibility flag — forming a bounded lattice L under the partial order of capability subsumption. Define a task specification T as a finite sequence of required tool signatures. Prove Theorem 1 (Minimal Grant): the meet ⋀{c ∈ L | c satisfies T} is the unique minimal permission set for T, computable in O(|T| · |L|) via a greedy lattice traversal. (2) Blast-Radius Bound: Model agent execution as a Markov Decision Process where states are (memory, permission-set, tool-call-history). Define blast radius B(π, s) as the maximum expected irreversible state change reachable under policy π from state s. Prove Theorem 2 (Blast-Radius Bound): for any agent policy π operating under permission set p, B(π, s) ≤ Σ_{t ∈ p} w(t) · d(t), where w(t) is the write-scope of tool t and d(t) is its irreversibility depth, both statically derivable from API schemas. (3) Sandboxing Sufficiency Condition: Define a sandbox S as sufficient for task T iff S contains the minimal grant from Theorem 1 and B(π, s) < ε for a user-specified ε. Provide a constructive algorithm (Algorithm 1: MinSandbox) that, given an OpenAPI 3.0 schema and a task description, outputs a sufficient sandbox configuration as a JSON policy file consumable by existing runtimes (AWS Lambda resource policies, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, or LangChain tool wrappers). Proof sketches use standard lattice theory and MDP reachability analysis. Validate the framework by manually annotating 50 real agent task traces from the ToolBench benchmark (existing public dataset, 16k tool-use trajectories) with ground-truth minimal permission sets, then measuring how often MinSandbox produces a superset (soundness) and how close it is to minimal (precision = |MinSandbox output| / |ground truth|).
baselines: full-permission grants, manual allow-lists from two open-source agent frameworks (LangChain v0.2, AutoGPT v5)
expected_outcome: A peer-reviewable theoretical framework with two proved theorems and one constructive algorithm; an open-source Python library (<500 LOC) implementing MinSandbox that ingests OpenAPI schemas and outputs JSON policy files; empirical validation showing ≥95% soundness and ≥80% precision on ToolBench; a 5-page technical report deployable as an arXiv preprint within 6 months. Practitioners can drop MinSandbox into existing LangChain or AWS Lambda deployments with zero model changes.
title: Minimal Permission Lattices for LLM Tool-Use Agents: A Formal Trust Calculus

provenance.resources_text: ToolBench dataset (public, https://github.com/OpenBMB/ToolBench, ~16k traces, 3500 APIs); OpenAPI 3.0 schema parser (openapi-core Python library); LangChain v0.2 and AutoGPT v5 source for baseline comparison; 1 engineer-month for annotation of 50 traces; standard laptop compute (no GPU required — purely symbolic/formal analysis); LaTeX for theorem write-up.

topic: agents with llms
motivation: VLM Q-learning (Cluster B) treats the vision-language model as a monolithic policy, ignoring the compositional skill structure emerging from Cluster A research (SSL representation: Scheduling-Structural-Logical). Without a formal account of how skill modularity interacts with Q-value propagation, practitioners cannot predict when RL fine-tuning will converge, diverge, or reward-hack. A theoretical framework bridging skill-graph topology and Bellman contraction properties would give provable sample-complexity bounds and misalignment risk certificates—something neither cluster provides alone.
hypothesis: For a VLM agent whose skill repertoire is organized as a DAG of depth d>=2, the skill-decomposed Bellman operator converges to the optimal Q-function in at most N(epsilon,delta,d) samples—strictly fewer than the flat (d=1) bound—and the misalignment risk R_hack remains below a computable threshold when the RL learning rate satisfies the derived sufficient condition; empirically, on ALFWorld with GPT-2-medium+DQN, depth-3 skill DAGs will reach 90% task success in at most 40% fewer environment steps than the flat VLM Q-learning baseline.
method_sketch: 1. FORMALIZATION: Model an agent's skill repertoire as a directed acyclic graph G=(V,E) where each node v_i encodes a SSL skill triple (s_i, l_i, c_i) for scheduling, logical, and contextual components. Define a skill-conditioned Q-function Q_pi(s,a,v_i) that decomposes the standard Bellman operator T into a composition of per-skill Bellman sub-operators T_{v_i}. 2. CONTRACTION ANALYSIS: Prove that the composed operator T_G = T_{v_k} ∘ ... ∘ T_{v_1} is a gamma^d contraction in the L-infinity norm, where d is the longest dependency path in G and gamma in (0,1) is the discount factor. Theorem 1 (sketch): For a skill DAG of depth d and discount gamma, ||T_G Q - T_G Q'||_inf <= gamma^d ||Q - Q'||_inf, yielding tighter contraction than the flat gamma bound when d>=2. 3. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY BOUND: Using the contraction result, derive a PAC-style sample complexity bound N(epsilon, delta, d) = O((|V| * H^2 * log(|V|/delta)) / (epsilon^2 * (1-gamma^d)^2)) where H is the horizon, showing that deeper skill decomposition (larger d) reduces required samples by a factor of (1-gamma)^2/(1-gamma^d)^2. 4. REWARD HACKING CERTIFICATE: Define a misalignment risk measure R_hack(G) = max_{v_i in V} KL(pi_RL(a|s,v_i) || pi_pretrain(a|s,v_i)) and prove that R_hack is bounded above by a function of the skill graph's out-degree and the RL learning rate eta, giving a sufficient condition eta < 2*(1-gamma^d)/(max_outdegree(G)*||grad log pi||_max) for misalignment-free convergence. 5. VALIDATION PROXY: Instantiate the framework on the ALFWorld benchmark (text-based interactive tasks, publicly available) using a GPT-2-medium backbone fine-tuned with DQN, measuring empirical convergence steps vs. theoretical bound N(0.05, 0.1, d) for skill DAGs of depth d in {1,2,3,4} constructed via the SSL decomposition heuristic from the 2026 paper.
metrics: task success, environment steps, misalignment risk R_hack, convergence steps
expected_outcome: (1) A proved Theorem establishing gamma^d contraction for skill-decomposed Bellman operators with explicit constants. (2) A closed-form sample complexity bound N(epsilon,delta,d) showing O((1-gamma)^2/(1-gamma^d)^2) improvement over flat baseline. (3) A sufficient condition on learning rate eta for bounded misalignment risk. (4) Empirical validation on ALFWorld showing depth-3 skill DAG reaches 90% success in <=60k environment steps vs >=100k for flat VLM Q-learning (GPT-2-medium+DQN), matching the theoretical prediction within a 15% margin.
title: Formal Convergence Bounds for Q-Value Estimation in Skill-Structured VLM Agents

topic: agents swarm and llms
motivation: LLM agents increasingly rely on case-based reasoning (CBR) to overcome hallucination and improve generalization, yet no standardized benchmark exists to measure how well CBR integration actually helps agents adapt to novel problems versus merely memorizing past solutions. Without such a dataset, claims about CBR-enhanced agents remain anecdotal. Bridging the LLM agent architecture literature (Cluster A) with CBR integration research (Cluster B) reveals a critical gap: we lack annotated traces that capture case retrieval decisions, adaptation steps, and outcome quality in realistic agent tasks—including safety-critical domains like infrastructure management.
hypothesis: LLM agents equipped with a full CBR pipeline (retrieve-adapt-retain) will outperform vanilla LLM agents by ≥10% on generalization-flagged instances (novel problems) as measured by task-specific F1/pass@1, while showing ≤3% improvement on memorization-flagged instances—demonstrating that CBR benefit is driven by genuine adaptation rather than lookup, and that this effect is measurable via the proposed dataset's generalization/memorization split.
method_sketch: 1. TASK DOMAIN SELECTION: Curate 4 domains from existing benchmarks—(a) educational tutoring dialogs from CogEvolution-style simulations, (b) water distribution optimization scenarios inspired by WaterAdmin, (c) code debugging from HumanEval-plus, and (d) multi-hop QA from HotpotQA. Each domain contributes 500 problem instances (2,000 total). 2. CASE LIBRARY CONSTRUCTION: For each problem instance, construct a case library of 20–50 candidate past cases using GPT-4o to generate plausible prior solutions, then manually verify correctness via domain experts (3 annotators per case, Cohen's kappa ≥ 0.75 required). 3. ANNOTATION SCHEMA: Each dataset record contains: (a) problem description, (b) ground-truth case retrieval ranking (annotated by experts using BM25 + semantic similarity via text-embedding-3-large as retrieval candidates), (c) required adaptation operations labeled from a fixed taxonomy (direct-reuse, parameter-substitution, structural-modification, rejection), (d) final solution quality scored on task-specific metrics (F1 for QA, pass@1 for code, constraint-satisfaction-rate for optimization), and (e) a 'generalization flag' indicating whether the problem is a near-duplicate of a case (memorization test) or genuinely novel (generalization test). 4. QUALITY CONTROL: Automated consistency checks reject cases where annotator agreement < 0.75; adversarial perturbation tests (swap 20% of case libraries with irrelevant cases) validate retrieval annotation robustness. 5. BASELINE EVALUATION: Evaluate 4 systems on the dataset—(a) vanilla GPT-4o with no CBR, (b) GPT-4o + BM25 retrieval, (c) GPT-4o + dense retrieval (FAISS + text-embedding-3-large), (d) GPT-4o + a full CBR pipeline (retrieve-adapt-retain loop using LangChain's memory module). Report retrieval NDCG@5, adaptation accuracy (exact-match against annotated operation type), and final task F1/pass@1/constraint-satisfaction.
metrics: retrieval NDCG@5, adaptation accuracy, final task F1/pass@1/constraint-satisfaction
baselines: vanilla GPT-4o with no CBR, GPT-4o + BM25 retrieval, GPT-4o + dense retrieval (FAISS + text-embedding-3-large), GPT-4o + a full CBR pipeline (retrieve-adapt-retain loop using LangChain's memory module)
expected_outcome: A publicly released dataset (CBR-AgentBench) of 2,000 annotated agent decision traces across 4 domains, with a companion leaderboard. Baseline results showing that current dense-retrieval CBR agents achieve ~12% F1 gain over vanilla GPT-4o on novel problems but only ~2% on near-duplicate problems, validating the generalization/memorization split as a meaningful evaluation axis. A published annotation guideline and inter-annotator agreement report (target kappa ≥ 0.75). Dataset diversity analysis reporting domain balance, case library size distribution, and adaptation operation frequency histograms.
title: CBR-AgentBench: A Dataset for Evaluating Case-Based Reasoning Integration in LLM Agents Across Multi-Domain Decision Tasks

topic: CHAT WITH NEW NOVELTY IDEAS WITH AGENTS
motivation: The gap between scalable communication topologies (Cayley graphs) and formal agent communication calculi (?ACP) prevents the design of verifiable, efficient multi-agent systems. A tool library that integrates both would enable rigorous verification of topological properties and communication protocols.
hypothesis: Using CayleyGraphACPLib, we can automatically verify that a Cayley-optimized communication topology satisfies ?ACP safety and liveness properties with at least 90% reduction in verification time compared to manual analysis, while maintaining scalability to 1000 agents.
method_sketch: Develop a Python library (CayleyGraphACPLib) that provides: (1) Cayley graph generation and optimization for agent topologies (e.g., using networkx and custom solvers), (2) translation of ?ACP process terms into graph constraints, (3) model checking of ?ACP specifications on Cayley graphs via a custom SMT-based verifier (using Z3), (4) API for simulation and benchmark against existing tools (e.g., UPPAAL, PRISM). The library will include documentation, tutorials, and a benchmark suite with 10 topologies (ring, hypercube, etc.) and 5 ?ACP protocols.
expected_outcome: A reusable library with verified correctness; quantitative results showing >80% reduction in verification time for topologies up to 1000 nodes; 5% performance improvement in agent task completion due to optimal topology.

topic: agents with llms
motivation: LLM agents are being deployed in both precision scientific workflows (AFM automation, quantum simulations, atomistic modeling) and industrial physical control systems (modular production, parametric CAD, dynamics control). Both clusters share a critical unsolved problem: agents must self-correct when their natural-language reasoning produces outputs that violate hard physical constraints (force limits, convergence thresholds, safety interlocks, geometric feasibility). Yet no benchmark exists that jointly evaluates self-correction fidelity across both domains, and no study has empirically characterized whether self-correction mechanisms that work in one domain transfer to the other. This gap means practitioners deploying LLM agents in safety-critical physical systems have no principled basis for choosing architectures or trusting outputs.
hypothesis: The CGC architecture (D) will achieve a Constraint Satisfaction Rate at least 18 percentage points higher than Reflexion (B) on scientific tasks and at least 15 percentage points higher on industrial control tasks, because typed constraint schemas provide verifiable grounding that verbal self-reflection cannot. Furthermore, CDTS for CGC will exceed 0.60 (moderate positive correlation), indicating that structured constraint injection generalizes across physical domains, whereas Reflexion's CDTS will be below 0.30, confirming that verbal self-reflection is domain-brittle.
method_sketch: We construct PhysAgent-Bench, a two-domain benchmark with 400 tasks (200 scientific, 200 industrial control) and run a controlled empirical study across five self-correction architectures. Scientific domain tasks are drawn from: (1) AFM parameter optimization scenarios derived from the open-source NFFA-EUROPE AFM dataset and synthetic extensions, requiring agents to set scan parameters without exceeding cantilever force thresholds; (2) quantum many-body Hamiltonian configuration tasks using PySCF and QuSpin where agents must propose configurations that satisfy energy convergence within 1e-6 Hartree; (3) LAMMPS atomistic simulation input generation where agents must avoid unphysical interatomic distances. Industrial control tasks are drawn from: (1) IEC 61131-3 PLC ladder logic generation for modular production cell scenarios (using the open OpenPLC dataset and synthetic fault-injection extensions); (2) parametric 3D model constraint satisfaction using FreeCAD Python API where agents must generate valid CSG trees; (3) PID controller tuning tasks on the OpenAI Gym continuous control suite (HalfCheetah-v4, Pendulum-v1) where agents propose gain schedules that must satisfy Bode stability margins. The five self-correction architectures evaluated are: (A) Vanilla ReAct (baseline, no explicit self-correction); (B) Reflexion with verbal self-reflection; (C) CRITIC with external tool-based verification (physics simulator as oracle); (D) a novel Constraint-Grounded Correction (CGC) architecture we introduce, where a structured constraint schema (JSON-LD encoding physical invariants per task type) is injected into the agent's context at each correction step, and violations are surfaced as typed error objects rather than free-text feedback; (E) CGC augmented with a lightweight domain-specific verifier fine-tuned on 5k constraint-violation examples per domain using LoRA on Llama-3-70B. All architectures use GPT-4o as the base LLM except architecture E's verifier. Controlled variables: base LLM fixed to GPT-4o (temperature 0.0), maximum 5 self-correction rounds per task, identical tool access (Python subprocess, simulator APIs). Independent variable: self-correction architecture. Primary metrics: Constraint Satisfaction Rate (CSR) = fraction of final outputs satisfying all hard physical constraints; Correction Efficiency (CE) = mean rounds to first valid output; Cross-Domain Transfer Score (CDTS) = Pearson correlation of per-task CSR between scientific and industrial splits for each architecture. Statistical significance tested via paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test with Bonferroni correction across all architecture pairs (alpha=0.05). We also measure failure mode distribution using a taxonomy of 6 error types (unit confusion, boundary violation, convergence failure, geometric infeasibility, safety interlock breach, hallucinated parameter) coded by two independent annotators (Cohen's kappa target > 0.75).
dataset: NFFA-EUROPE AFM open dataset, OpenPLC open dataset, synthetic extensions generated via PySCF, QuSpin, LAMMPS, FreeCAD Python API, OpenAI Gym
metrics: Constraint Satisfaction Rate, Correction Efficiency, Cross-Domain Transfer Score
baselines: Vanilla ReAct, Reflexion with verbal self-reflection, CRITIC with external tool-based verification, CGC architecture, CGC augmented with a lightweight domain-specific verifier fine-tuned on 5k constraint-violation examples per domain using LoRA on Llama-3-70B
expected_outcome: We expect CGC (architecture D) to reach CSR of approximately 74% on scientific tasks and 71% on industrial tasks versus Reflexion baselines of approximately 54% and 54% respectively, exceeding the 18/15 pp hypothesis thresholds. Architecture E (CGC+verifier) is expected to reach 80%+ CSR on both domains. CDTS for CGC predicted at 0.65 vs 0.22 for Reflexion. Failure mode analysis expected to show that 'hallucinated parameter' errors drop by >40% under CGC versus Reflexion, while 'unit confusion' errors are the residual dominant failure mode, motivating future work on unit-aware constraint schemas. The benchmark, all task definitions, constraint schemas, and evaluation code will be released publicly.

topic: Developing an AI system for health guidance and clinical decision support in pediatrics using large language models.
motivation: Pediatric health data is sensitive and requires stringent privacy protections. Federated learning combined with Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC) can decentralize data storage and enable collaborative AI model training while preserving privacy. This research aims to enhance pediatric health decisions through a secure and ethically compliant AI framework.
hypothesis: The federated learning model integrated with SMPC will achieve at least 85% of the performance of a central model while significantly enhancing privacy and ethical compliance. Additionally, the model will demonstrate clinical efficacy and safety suitable for pediatric health decisions when validated with real-world data.
method_sketch: We will develop a federated learning framework for pediatric health data integrated with SMPC. The framework will enable AI models to learn from decentralized health records without transferring raw data. Our approach includes: 1) Designing a federated learning protocol that incorporates SMPC for secure model updates and aggregation using advanced cryptographic protocols like Homomorphic Encryption and Zero-Knowledge Proofs. 2) Implementing a pediatric health dataset simulator to mimic real-world data heterogeneity and distribution. 3) Collaborating with medical institutions to obtain real-world pediatric health data for validation. 4) Evaluating the system's performance, privacy preservation, and clinical efficacy using metrics such as accuracy, communication overhead, HIPAA compliance, and clinical assessment scores.
dataset: Pediatric health datasets (e.g., i2b2 dataset), simulated dataset for data heterogeneity and distribution, real-world pediatric health data from collaborating medical institutions
metrics: accuracy, communication overhead, HIPAA compliance, clinical assessment scores
expected_outcome: The federated learning model will achieve at least 85% of the performance of a central model trained on the same data while maintaining data local to each institution. The system will demonstrate reduced communication overhead and compliance with HIPAA and GDPR. Clinical efficacy and safety assessments using real-world data will support the model's applicability in real-world pediatric health decisions.
title: Secure Federated Learning for Pediatric Health: Integrating SMPC and Clinical Validation

topic: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for Stress Field Prediction in Irregular Structures
motivation: Stress field prediction in irregular structures such as geological formations and architectural structures is crucial for safety and stability. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have shown promise in these tasks, but their performance can be improved by integrating topological insights. This integration can provide GNNs with a deeper understanding of the spatial relationships in the data, leading to more accurate predictions.
hypothesis: The hypothesis is that the proposed GNN architecture, which integrates topological features extracted using persistent homology, will outperform state-of-the-art GNN models in stress field prediction tasks.
method_sketch: We will conduct a survey meta-analysis to systematically review and categorize existing research on stress field prediction using GNNs. We will then develop a novel GNN architecture that incorporates topological features extracted using persistent homology, a technique from Topological Data Analysis (TDA). The architecture will be evaluated on a benchmark dataset of stress fields in geological formations, which we will make publicly available to ensure reproducibility. The evaluation will involve comparing the performance of the proposed GNN architecture with state-of-the-art models.
dataset: benchmark dataset of stress fields in geological formations
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is the development of a novel GNN architecture that integrates topological insights for stress field prediction. We expect this model to outperform state-of-the-art GNN models in terms of accuracy and robustness. Additionally, we expect to provide a comprehensive review of existing research in this area, which will help identify open problems and future research directions.
title: Enhancing Stress Field Prediction in Irregular Structures through Topological Insights Integration

topic: 1. Stigmergic LLM Agents (no message passing). Almost every multi-agent LLM paper assumes explicit message passing. Borrow stigmergy from ant colonies: agents only modify a shared external workspace (annotated artifacts, "pheromone" weights on a graph), never speak to each other. Show that emergent coordination beats explicit communication on long-horizon tasks because it removes the linguistic bottleneck. The novelty is denying communication is necessary at all.
motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems rely on explicit inter-agent messaging, creating communication bottlenecks and tight coupling. Global Workspace Theory (GWT) offers a broadcast-based coordination model, while stigmergy offers indirect coordination through shared artifact modification. Combining these eliminates direct message-passing while preserving coherent task execution—addressing the open question of whether stigmergic workspaces can replace explicit messaging without coherence loss.
hypothesis: StigmaGW agents will achieve within 5% of explicit message-passing baseline on HotPotQA multi-hop reasoning (F1 score) and SWE-bench Lite (task completion rate) while reducing inter-agent token exchange by at least 60%, with CCM repair tokens preventing coherence degradation on tasks requiring >5 sequential coordination steps.
method_sketch: Design StigmaGW, a system with three concrete components: (1) a Structured Artifact Workspace (SAW) — a versioned JSON-LD document store where agents read/write typed 'pheromone tokens' (task-state tuples with fields: agent_id, action_type, confidence_score, causal_parent_id, expiry_ttl); (2) a Global Workspace Broadcaster (GWB) — a lightweight attention-based router (2-layer transformer, 64-dim) that selects the top-k (k=3) highest-salience artifact modifications per tick and broadcasts them to all agents as a shared 'spotlight' context window prepended to each agent's next prompt; (3) a Causal Coherence Monitor (CCM) — a causal graph maintained over artifact edits using the DoWhy library, which detects coordination failures (cycles, orphaned edits) and injects corrective 'repair tokens' into the SAW. Agents are LLaMA-3-8B instances with no direct inter-agent communication channels. System diagram: Agents → write pheromone tokens → SAW → GWB selects top-k by salience score → broadcasts spotlight context → Agents read spotlight + local SAW view → CCM monitors causal graph → injects repair tokens on failure. Baseline comparison: same LLaMA-3-8B agents with explicit JSON message-passing (no SAW/GWB).
expected_outcome: StigmaGW achieves F1 ≥ 58 on HotPotQA (baseline explicit messaging ~61 F1) and ≥ 18% task completion on SWE-bench Lite (baseline ~19%), with ≥60% reduction in total tokens exchanged between agents. CCM repair token injection reduces coordination failure rate from an estimated 25% to <10% on tasks with >5 coordination steps.
title: StigmaGW: A Global Workspace Architecture for Stigmergic Multi-Agent LLM Coordination

provenance.resources_text: Datasets: HotPotQA (publicly available, multi-hop QA), SWE-bench Lite (publicly available, 300 GitHub issues). Compute: 4x A100 80GB GPUs for running 4 parallel LLaMA-3-8B agents. Software: LLaMA-3-8B via HuggingFace transformers, DoWhy 0.11 for causal graph, JSON-LD via PyLD, custom GWB implemented in PyTorch. Estimated runtime: 72 hours for full evaluation.

topic: 1. Stigmergic LLM Agents (no message passing). Almost every multi-agent LLM paper assumes explicit message passing. Borrow stigmergy from ant colonies: agents only modify a shared external workspace (annotated artifacts, "pheromone" weights on a graph), never speak to each other. Show that emergent coordination beats explicit communication on long-horizon tasks because it removes the linguistic bottleneck. The novelty is denying communication is necessary at all.
motivation: The frontier paper's GWT architecture uses tree-structured deliberation but assumes agents can negotiate branching decisions through dialogue. The open question is whether tree-structured deliberation remains effective when agents cannot negotiate. This work replaces dialogue-based branching with a shared artifact representation where the deliberation tree itself is the coordination medium: agents vote on branches by writing scored sub-artifacts, and a deterministic arbitration rule selects expansions.
hypothesis: Artifact-mediated tree deliberation with deterministic arbitration will match or exceed dialogue-based tree search on MATH competition problems (target: within 5% of dialogue baseline accuracy) while reducing the number of LLM calls by at least 30%, because redundant negotiation turns are eliminated.
method_sketch: Represent the deliberation tree as a versioned JSON document in a shared workspace. Each node contains: a task description, a list of candidate expansions (each written by a different agent), and a score vector. Agents are assigned roles (proposer, critic, executor) and interact only by appending to or scoring nodes—never by sending messages. Implement a Condorcet-style arbitration function that selects the next branch to expand based on aggregate scores, breaking ties by recency. Build on top of the Theater of Mind GWT codebase (if open-sourced) or reimplement the core workspace in Python with a FastAPI coordination server. Evaluate on MATH reasoning tasks and WebArena web-agent tasks, measuring branching quality (solution correctness) and deliberation efficiency (nodes expanded per correct solution).
metrics: solution correctness, nodes expanded per correct solution
expected_outcome: A concrete demonstration that branching decisions in tree-structured deliberation can be made through artifact scoring alone, with a quantified comparison of solution quality and compute cost against a dialogue-negotiation baseline, plus an analysis of failure modes (e.g., tasks where score aggregation produces poor branch selection).
title: ArtifactTree: Tree-Structured Deliberation via Shared Representational Artifacts Without Agent Dialogue

provenance.resources_text: Open-source LLMs (GPT-4o-mini or Llama-3 70B via API), Python FastAPI for coordination server, MATH dataset (publicly available), WebArena benchmark (publicly available), standard cloud compute.

topic: agents swarm and llms to generative novelty research papers ideas
motivation: Multi-agent systems for research gap detection conflate three distinct sources of performance gain—prompt specialization, architectural heterogeneity, and retrieval augmentation—because prior work either uses identical backbones with role prompts or bundles all three factors simultaneously. This makes mechanistic claims untestable and prevents principled system design. Additionally, existing evaluation protocols suffer from circular validity (the same experts who define the gold standard also rate outputs) and capability confounds (comparing GPT-4o against 2-3B parameter models). This work introduces a clean 5-condition factorial design with capability-matched backbones and a disjoint holdout cohort for ontology validation, enabling the first causally interpretable decomposition of what drives gap detection quality.
hypothesis: Condition C (full system) will achieve Recall@3 at least 12 percentage points above Condition D (homogeneous baseline) and at least 6 percentage points above Condition A (prompt-only), with the architecture effect (E vs A) contributing at least 4 percentage points independently of prompts and retrieval, Cohen's d > 0.5 on primary metric, and Krippendorff's alpha > 0.6 on Cohort B human ratings. Ontology recall against Cohort B holdout will exceed 0.65, establishing that the gold standard generalizes beyond its constructors.
method_sketch: 1) GROUND TRUTH CONSTRUCTION (pre-registered, prospective, two-cohort design): Cohort A (15 researchers: 8 CS-NLP, 7 Biomedical-NLP) independently enumerates 5-10 open problems each via structured elicitation (problem statement, why unsolved, partial solutions), recruited via ACL/EMNLP/BioNLP mailing lists. Aggregate into gap ontology using hierarchical clustering on all-mpnet-base-v2 embeddings, yielding ~40 canonical gap nodes per domain, reviewed and merged by two senior researchers. Cohort B (10 researchers: 5 CS-NLP, 5 Biomedical-NLP, zero overlap with Cohort A) independently elicits gaps using the same form. Ontology recall against Cohort B is computed (cosine similarity > 0.75) BEFORE any swarm conditions run, establishing ontology coverage ceiling and generalizability bound. Cohort B is then used exclusively as human raters, eliminating circular validity. 2) BACKBONE CAPABILITY MATCHING: All agent roles use models of comparable capability tier. Generator Agent: GPT-4o with FAISS RAG (10K domain abstracts from S2ORC/PubMed-OA, top-5 retrieval). Critic Agent: GPT-4o with adversarial chain-of-thought, no retrieval. Domain Expert Agent: GPT-4o-mini fine-tuned on domain corpora via OpenAI fine-tuning API (same S2ORC/PubMed-OA abstracts as RAG index, ~5K examples per domain, 3 epochs). This preserves architectural heterogeneity (different training distribution and specialization) while controlling for raw capability, making architecture-vs-prompt decomposition interpretable. 3) FIVE-CONDITION FACTORIAL DESIGN: Condition D — homogeneous baseline: single GPT-4o, no roles, no RAG. Condition A — prompt-only: all GPT-4o, role prompts, no RAG, no fine-tuned model. Condition B — architecture-only: heterogeneous backbones (GPT-4o + GPT-4o + GPT-4o-mini-FT), uniform generic prompts, no RAG. Condition E — architecture + prompts, no RAG: heterogeneous backbones + role prompts, no RAG. Condition C — full system: heterogeneous backbones + role prompts + RAG on Generator. This enables clean decomposition: prompt effect = A vs D; architecture effect = E vs A; retrieval effect = C vs E; interaction = C vs (A + E - D). 4) PROTOCOL: Each condition processes 60 paper clusters (30 CS-NLP, 30 Biomedical-NLP), each cluster = 5 abstracts from S2ORC/PubMed-OA filtered to citation count 10-100. Swarm runs 3 debate rounds per cluster, producing 3 gap hypotheses per cluster. 5) HUMAN EVALUATION (Cohort B only): Stratified random sample of 60 clusters. Each cluster assigned to 3 raters from Cohort B (same domain). Maximum 15 items per session, mandatory 24h break between sessions. Raters score Novelty (1-5), Feasibility (1-5), Specificity (1-5) using pre-registered blind rubric. IRB computed via Krippendorff's alpha; alpha < 0.6 triggers recalibration. 3 anchor items per session (pre-rated by PI) detect calibration drift; raters showing >1.5 SD drift excluded and replaced. 6) AUTOMATED METRICS: Recall@K against gap ontology nodes (cosine similarity > 0.75, K=3), Distinct-2/3 for diversity, Jensen-Shannon divergence on token distributions between Critic and Generator outputs to verify behavioral specialization. 7) STATISTICS: Mixed-effects ANOVA across 5 conditions × 2 domains with cluster as random effect; post-hoc Tukey HSD; Cohen's d effect sizes; ontology recall against Cohort B reported as validity bound.
dataset: S2ORC CS-NLP subset, PubMed Central Open Access biomedical-NLP subset
metrics: Recall@K, Distinct-2/3, Jensen-Shannon divergence, Novelty, Feasibility, Specificity, ontology recall
baselines: homogeneous baseline, prompt-only, architecture-only, architecture + prompts, no RAG

topic: agents swarm and llms to generative novelty research papers ideas
motivation: LLM agent pipelines generate research ideas at scale, but automated quality signals remain unreliable: embedding-based novelty metrics conflate semantic drift with genuine advancement, and feasibility proxies based on peer-review outcomes are confounded by prestige rather than resource constraints. Prior work (CGNS) exposed two deeper failure modes: perplexity gates penalize unconventional phrasing rather than conceptual incoherence, and hand-engineered resource vectors impose false geometric commensurability on qualitatively distinct constraints. Fixing these failure modes requires replacing heuristic proxies with learned discriminative functions trained on grounded supervision.
hypothesis: CDNS novelty scores will achieve Spearman rho >= 0.65 with Dawid-Skene latent true novelty scores across 500 evaluated ideas, significantly outperforming ungated SPECTER2 (expected rho ~0.50), BM25 (expected rho ~0.35), and perplexity-gated CGNS (expected rho ~0.55, Bonferroni-corrected p<0.05 for 3 comparisons). The learned resource compatibility cross-encoder will achieve Spearman rho >= 0.60 with human feasibility ratings, outperforming the cosine-over-3D-vector baseline (expected rho ~0.40). The contrastive coherence classifier will achieve F1 >= 0.80 on a held-out 1k-pair validation set, and ideas it rejects will receive >= 0.8 Likert points lower human novelty ratings than passed ideas (Mann-Whitney U, p<0.05).
method_sketch: We propose CDNS (Contrastive Domain-grounded Novelty Scorer) with three redesigned components. (1) Contrastive Coherence Classifier: Train a SPECTER2-based binary classifier to distinguish domain-grounded ideas from superficially fluent but conceptually misaligned ones. Positive pairs: (abstract-derived idea, correct domain label) sampled from S2ORC. Hard negatives: (abstract-derived idea, wrong domain label) using in-batch negatives across 5 domains (NLP, CV, RL, BioML, HCI). Fine-tune on 50k pairs per domain (250k total) with cross-entropy loss; threshold at 0.5 posterior probability. This targets conceptual alignment rather than surface fluency, avoiding the perplexity gate's bias against unconventional phrasing. (2) Coherence-Gated Novelty Score: For ideas passing the classifier, compute cosine distance between the idea's SPECTER2 embedding and its k=20 nearest neighbors in a domain-filtered FAISS IVF-PQ index (~5M papers per domain from S2ORC). Gating ensures high distance reflects genuine conceptual departure. (3) Learned Resource Compatibility Function: Fine-tune a DeBERTa-v3-base cross-encoder on (methods_text, resource_profile_text) pairs. Supervision: S2ORC papers with extractable compute/dataset metadata (GPU counts via regex over Methods sections, dataset names via entity linking to PapersWithCode dataset registry). Label: binary — did the paper report successful execution given its stated resource profile? Construct 30k positive pairs (paper executed successfully) and 30k hard negatives (resource profile swapped with a paper from a higher compute tier in the same domain). This learns non-linear resource compatibility from data, avoiding geometric incommensurability of hand-engineered vectors. (4) Hierarchical Bayesian Annotation: Replace flat Krippendorff threshold with Dawid-Skene model estimating per-rater reliability and bias parameters. Use posterior latent true scores as gold standard for Spearman correlation analysis. Report per-rater posterior reliability distributions; rater disagreement becomes an informative signal about idea ambiguity rather than noise to be thresholded away. Human evaluation: 100 ideas per domain (500 total), 5 PhD-level raters per domain recruited via Prolific with subfield screening (raters must list >= 3 publications in the target domain). Power analysis (rho=0.65, alpha=0.05, power=0.80) yields N=67 minimum; 100 per domain provides buffer. Baselines: (A) ungated SPECTER2 distance, (B) BM25 overlap against S2ORC titles, (C) original perplexity-gated CGNS as ablation.
