topic: An Agent of Extraordinary Intelligence
motivation: Current AI evaluation lacks rigorous causal metrics for extraordinary intelligence—the ability to transfer core reasoning principles across fundamentally different domains with minimal data. This gap conflates memorization with genuine understanding, impeding AGI progress. We address this by developing a causal framework that isolates transfer mechanisms from confounding factors like dataset biases, using randomized designs to ensure valid inference and synthetic tasks with realistic noise to enhance ecological validity.
hypothesis: Models exhibiting extraordinary intelligence will show significantly higher CTE scores compared to all baseline models (p<0.01 after multiple testing correction), with CTE positively correlated with human transfer efficiency on analogous tasks (statistically significant correlation, no arbitrary threshold). Transfer will be robust across domain pairs, and attention patterns will reveal increased cross-domain weights for principle-relevant features, consistent with theoretical predictions from cognitive architectures.
method_sketch: We propose an empirical study with three phases: (1) Dataset Creation: Generate synthetic 'Noisy Cognitive Gradient' tasks across 5 domains (visual, textual, symbolic, numerical, procedural) with controlled complexity levels (e.g., pattern completion, logical deduction). Each task embeds shared underlying reasoning principles (e.g., symmetry, transitivity) but includes realistic noise (e.g., ambiguous features, adversarial perturbations) and minimal, orthogonal surface features across domains. Validate tasks with human participants performing analogous versions to ensure cognitive relevance. (2) Experimental Design: Implement a randomized controlled trial where source domains are randomly assigned to different complexity levels (low, medium, high) for each model run. Train small transformer models from scratch using meta-learning (MAML) with ≤10 examples per task. Evaluate zero-shot transfer to target domains with identical principles but orthogonal features. Use structural causal modeling (SCM) with do-calculus to estimate causal transfer efficiency: explicitly model confounders (e.g., model architecture, training dynamics) and apply do-interventions on source domain complexity to isolate causal effects from memorization. (3) Analysis: Compute Causal Transfer Efficiency (CTE) as (target performance - baseline) / (causal effect of source complexity), with statistical significance assessed via bootstrapped confidence intervals and cross-validation across multiple synthetic task families. Compare against diverse baselines: random models, memorization-based models (trained on scrambled principles), and state-of-the-art meta-learners. Analyze attention patterns for alignment with cognitive theories.
dataset: Synthetic Noisy Cognitive Gradient tasks, human validation set
baselines: random models, memorization-based models, state-of-the-art meta-learners
expected_outcome: (1) A validated Causal Transfer Efficiency (CTE) metric with demonstrated reliability (Cronbach's α > 0.8) across domain pairs and task families. (2) Empirical CTE scores for transformer models, showing statistically significant superiority over baselines. (3) Causal evidence of transfer via SCM with sensitivity analyses. (4) Open-source benchmark suite (synthetic tasks + evaluation code) for reproducible assessment of extraordinary intelligence, including human performance data for ecological validation.
title: Benchmarking Causal Transfer Learning via Randomized Domain Assignment on Noisy Cognitive Tasks

topic: An Agent of Extraordinary Intelligence
motivation: Current AI systems lack efficient mechanisms for autonomously integrating knowledge across heterogeneous domains (e.g., text, vision, robotics) without extensive human intervention or catastrophic forgetting. This limits the development of generalist agents capable of adapting to diverse real-world tasks. The core challenge is designing scalable learning algorithms that enable meaningful cross-domain knowledge transfer while maintaining computational efficiency and avoiding domain interference.
hypothesis: The hierarchical meta-learning framework with dynamic gating will achieve significantly higher Cross-Domain Adaptation Scores (CDAS) and faster convergence compared to baselines, due to its ability to efficiently adapt domain-specific modules while minimizing redundancy through the gating mechanism and redundancy penalty.
method_sketch: A three-stage empirical study: (1) Design a hierarchical meta-learning framework with two levels: (a) intra-domain adaptation using gradient-based hypernetworks to generate domain-specific module parameters, and (b) cross-domain transfer via a dynamic gating mechanism that computes attention weights over domain modules based on input features, optimized with a novel loss function combining task performance and a redundancy penalty term (L_redundancy = -λ * Σ_i≠j cos_sim(module_i, module_j)). (2) Implement this using a modular architecture with pre-trained domain experts (BERT for text, ResNet-50 for vision, SAC for control) as base modules. (3) Evaluate on a newly curated unified multimodal benchmark 'CrossDomain-MM' comprising 50k synthetic examples combining text descriptions, corresponding images, and robotic action sequences (e.g., 'pick up the red block' paired with an image and MuJoCo trajectory). Performance measured via a unified metric: Cross-Domain Adaptation Score (CDAS) = 0.4*BLEU + 0.3*top-1 accuracy + 0.3*normalized return. Baselines include: (i) monolithic GPT-3 fine-tuning per domain, (ii) naive multi-task learning, (iii) MAML-based modular approach. Controlled variables: fixed 1200 GPU-hour budget, 70/15/15 dataset split, hyperparameter optimization via Bayesian search. Statistical significance assessed with ANOVA (p<0.01).
dataset: CrossDomain-MM, GLUE subset for validation, ImageNet-1K subset, MuJoCo environments (Hopper, Walker2d)
metrics: Cross-Domain Adaptation Score (CDAS), BLEU, top-1 accuracy, normalized return

expected_outcome: Quantitative results: (1) 8-10% improvement in CDAS over GPT-3 fine-tuning baseline; (2) 25% reduction in training time to reach 95% of maximum performance; (3) statistically significant differences (p<0.01) in cross-domain transfer efficiency across all domains. Deliverables: open-source implementation of the hierarchical meta-learning framework, the CrossDomain-MM benchmark dataset, and reproducible experimental protocols.

topic: generative AI and agents
motivation: Autonomous agents operating across extended sessions (e.g., multi-day coding assistants, research agents) suffer catastrophic forgetting and context-window overflow. Current approaches—naive full-context, sliding-window, or flat vector retrieval—fail to preserve causal structure across episodes. No systematic empirical comparison exists on which memory architecture best supports long-horizon reasoning tasks requiring multi-step inference over facts acquired hours or sessions earlier.
hypothesis: HEM will outperform all baselines by at least 8% F1 on LoCoMo, 10% task success on AgentBench-LongTask, and 12% exact-match on LHCB, with the semantic graph tier (HEM vs HEM-NoGraph) accounting for at least 40% of the gain over Flat-RAG, demonstrating that hierarchical abstraction—not just retrieval—is the critical factor for long-horizon reasoning.
method_sketch: We implement and compare four memory architectures on a unified agent scaffold (ReAct + GPT-4o backbone): (1) Baseline-Sliding: 8k-token sliding window; (2) Flat-RAG: FAISS-indexed dense retrieval (text-embedding-3-small embeddings, top-k=5); (3) HEM (proposed): a two-tier Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory where raw observations are compressed into episodic summaries every 20 turns via a summarization LLM call, then episodic summaries are further abstracted into a semantic knowledge graph (NetworkX, entity-relation triples extracted via GPT-4o structured output); retrieval at inference time queries both tiers and fuses results via a learned re-ranker (cross-encoder ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2); (4) HEM-NoGraph: ablation removing the semantic graph tier. All agents are evaluated on three benchmarks: (a) LoCoMo (long-context multi-session conversation QA, F1 metric), (b) AgentBench-LongTask (a 50-task subset requiring >10 sequential tool calls, task success rate), and (c) a synthetic Long-Horizon Coding Benchmark (LHCB) we construct from 30 GitHub repos requiring agents to recall design decisions made >50 turns prior (exact-match on 200 probe questions). Controlled variables: same LLM backbone, same tool set, same prompt templates except memory retrieval block. Statistical significance via paired bootstrap resampling (n=1000, p<0.05).
dataset: LoCoMo,AgentBench,30 open-source GitHub repos for LHCB construction
expected_outcome: HEM achieves ≥8% F1 improvement on LoCoMo over Flat-RAG (expected ~0.61 vs ~0.53), ≥10% absolute task success on AgentBench-LongTask (expected ~0.58 vs ~0.48), and ≥12% exact-match on LHCB (expected ~0.44 vs ~0.32). Ablation confirms semantic graph contributes ~5% F1 independently. We release HEM as an open-source library and the LHCB benchmark.
title: Hierarchical Episodic-Semantic Memory for Long-Horizon Reasoning in Autonomous Agents

topic: hero Agents with Large Language Models
motivation: LLM agents on long-horizon tasks fail primarily due to goal drift and context loss: GPT-4 ReAct achieves only 53% on ALFWorld and 14.9% on WebArena. Prior work conflates context compression with re-planning triggers into monolithic systems, preventing causal attribution. A controlled factorial decomposition with a model-independent coherence scorer and task-success-validated compression thresholds would establish which component drives improvement and under what conditions, providing causal clarity prerequisite for principled agent architecture design.
hypothesis: H1: Goal signature (factor A) independently improves ALFWorld long-horizon success rate by >= 6 pp over A-B- baseline (p<0.05, McNemar's). H2: Calibrated coherence re-planning (factor B) independently improves success by >= 4 pp over A-B- baseline (p<0.05, McNemar's). H3: The A×B interaction likelihood-ratio test yields a non-negative odds ratio (synergistic or additive), reported with 95% CI; primary test on WebArena (812 tasks, adequately powered), exploratory on ALFWorld. H4: The DistilBERT coherence classifier achieves 5-fold CV F1 >= 0.75 against human alignment labels on the 200-pair set, with a reliability diagram showing calibration error < 0.10.
method_sketch: 2x2 factorial design with four treatment conditions plus three baselines. Factors: A = Goal Signature (present/absent), B = Coherence Re-planning (enabled/disabled). Conditions: (1) A-B- rolling summary only, (2) A+B- goal signature + rolling summary no re-planning, (3) A-B+ rolling summary + re-planning no signature, (4) A+B+ full system. Baselines: (5) vanilla ReAct full history, (6) Plan-and-Execute LangChain, (7) sliding window last-5-turns. Goal signature compression threshold validation: for each budget in {25, 50, 75, 100} tokens, run condition A+B- on a 50-task held-out split (stratified by ALFWorld task type) and record success rate; select the minimum budget within 2 pp of the 100-token ceiling as the operating point; additionally report TF-IDF cosine similarity as a secondary diagnostic. This makes threshold selection task-success-grounded and falsifiable. Coherence scorer: collect 200 human-labeled (executed-action, goal-signature) pairs via two annotators (Cohen's kappa target >= 0.75); fine-tune a DistilBERT-base-uncased classifier on these pairs using 5-fold cross-validation; report held-out F1 and a reliability diagram (calibration curve); use this model-independent classifier as the re-planning trigger, eliminating circular self-evaluation by the backbone LLM. Rolling summary: 3-sentence compression call after each step, maintained independently of the goal signature. All conditions use GPT-4o-mini, temperature=0, identical system prompts except the memory mechanism. Pre-registered power analysis before running experiments: compute minimum detectable effect size (MDES) for the A×B interaction given each benchmark's sample size at beta=0.80, alpha=0.05; explicitly label AgentBench (57 tasks) results as exploratory/underpowered. Primary evaluation on ALFWorld 134 test games (6 task types, stratified randomization, per-type 95% Wilson CIs) and WebArena 812 tasks (self-hosted Docker, 3-run majority vote per task). Primary metric: task success rate. Secondary: goal drift score (human-annotated 100 trajectories), steps to completion, prompt token cost. Statistics: McNemar's test per main effect (alpha=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected for 4 pairwise main-effect comparisons); for H3 interaction, report likelihood-ratio test p-value with odds ratio and 95% CI rather than Bonferroni-corrected threshold to distinguish statistical from practical significance.
metrics: task success rate, goal drift score, steps to completion, prompt token cost
baselines: vanilla ReAct full history, Plan-and-Execute LangChain, sliding window last-5-turns
expected_outcome: A+B+ achieves 62±3% on ALFWorld long-horizon subset and 21±2% on WebArena vs. 53%/14.9% ReAct baselines. Factor A alone accounts for ~6 pp gain, factor B alone ~4 pp. DistilBERT coherence classifier achieves 5-fold CV F1=0.77±0.04 with calibration error <0.08. Compression threshold sweep selects 50-75 token budgets for ALFWorld navigation tasks and 75-100 tokens for WebArena form-filling tasks, with the success-rate curve confirming the knee point aligns with TF-IDF cosine >= 0.82. Token cost of A+B+ is <= 20% above sliding window baseline. Pre-registered power analysis confirms WebArena (812 tasks) is adequately powered for the interaction test (MDES ~3 pp at beta=0.80) while ALFWorld (134 tasks) is adequately powered only for main effects.
title: Factorial Decomposition of Goal Anchoring and Coherence-Triggered Re-Planning in LLM Agents with Model-Independent Scoring

topic: large language model reasoning
motivation: LLMs trained on observational text learn spurious correlations that mimic causal reasoning on surface benchmarks (e.g., COPA, CausalQA) but fail when causal structure is perturbed. No study has systematically isolated the causal/correlational dissociation using do-calculus-inspired interventional probes across model families, nor tested whether targeted counterfactual fine-tuning closes the gap without degrading correlational performance.
hypothesis: LLMs exhibit a statistically significant causal-interventional gap (Δ > 15 accuracy points on interventional COPA variants, p < 0.01) that is not present for human annotators (Δ < 3 points), and counterfactual fine-tuning on 10k CRASS+CausalBench examples reduces this gap by ≥ 40% (absolute) in Llama-3-70B without degrading standard COPA accuracy by more than 2 points.
method_sketch: Phase 1 — Diagnostic Benchmark Construction: Extend COPA (1000 items) and CausalQA with 500 matched triplets per dataset: (a) original causal query, (b) correlational surrogate (same surface form, spurious association), (c) interventional variant where the causal mechanism is blocked via do-operator paraphrase (e.g., 'Suppose we forcibly set X regardless of its usual causes; does Y still follow?'). Use Amazon Mechanical Turk (n=3 annotators per item, Cohen's κ > 0.7 acceptance threshold) to validate human ground truth. Phase 2 — Model Probing: Evaluate GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B, Mistral-7B, and Claude-3-Sonnet zero-shot and 5-shot on all three query types. Primary metric: accuracy delta Δ = acc(causal) − acc(interventional), measuring causal-interventional gap. Secondary: calibration (ECE), and chain-of-thought faithfulness scored via FactScore against a causal graph oracle. Phase 3 — Counterfactual Fine-Tuning (CFT): Fine-tune Llama-3-70B on 10k counterfactual reasoning examples constructed from CausalBench and the CRASS dataset using LoRA (rank=16, α=32, lr=2e-4, 3 epochs). Control condition: standard SFT on same volume of non-interventional causal examples. Phase 4 — Statistical Analysis: Paired t-tests (α=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected for 4 models × 3 conditions) on accuracy deltas. Effect sizes reported as Cohen's d. Ablation: vary CFT data volume (1k, 5k, 10k) to fit a learning curve and estimate saturation point.
expected_outcome: Quantified causal-interventional accuracy gap per model family; learning curves showing CFT data efficiency; evidence that CFT transfers to held-out causal benchmarks (e.g., CLadder, CLADDER-Symbolic) with ≥ 10-point improvement over SFT baseline; published benchmark triplets and fine-tuned LoRA adapter weights.
title: Causal vs. Correlational Reasoning Dissociation in LLMs: Intervention-Based Probing and Counterfactual Fine-Tuning

topic: large language model reasoning
motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting empirically improves LLM reasoning, yet lacks formal guarantees: models frequently introduce logical contradictions across reasoning steps, producing hallucinated conclusions that are locally fluent but globally inconsistent. No existing framework provides provable upper bounds on error propagation across multi-step deductive chains, leaving practitioners with no principled way to detect or bound reasoning failures before they compound.
hypothesis: LCS-guided backtracking CoT will achieve at least 8% higher exact-match accuracy than standard CoT on GSM8K and reduce annotated hallucination rate by at least 30% relative to self-consistency CoT on StrategyQA, while requiring fewer than 2x the inference calls of standard CoT on average.
method_sketch: We develop a formal framework treating a CoT trace as a directed acyclic graph G=(V,E) where each node v_i is a proposition and each edge encodes an inference step. We define a Logical Contraction Score (LCS) for each step as LCS(v_i, v_{i-1}) = 1 - P_model(v_i | v_{i-1}, context) / P_model(v_i | context), measuring how much the prior step actually constrains the next. We then prove three theorems: (T1) Propagation Bound — the probability of a globally consistent trace decays at most exponentially in the number of steps with rate governed by the minimum LCS across the chain; (T2) Detectability Threshold — there exists a computable threshold LCS* below which a step is statistically indistinguishable from hallucination under a null model of random token generation, derivable from the model's own token log-probabilities; (T3) Repair Convergence — a greedy backtracking algorithm that re-samples any step with LCS < LCS* converges to a locally consistent trace in O(k^2) steps where k is chain length, under mild Lipschitz continuity assumptions on the model's conditional distributions. Proof sketches use measure-theoretic probability and information-theoretic arguments (KL divergence bounds). Empirically, we implement LCS computation using GPT-4o and Llama-3-70B log-probabilities on GSM8K (math word problems, 8,500 examples), MATH (competition math, 12,500 examples), and StrategyQA (multi-hop reasoning, 2,780 examples). We compare: (A) standard CoT, (B) self-consistency CoT with majority voting over 40 samples, (C) our LCS-guided backtracking CoT. Metrics: exact-match accuracy, hallucination rate (measured by human annotation on 500 sampled traces), and average chain length at convergence.
metrics: exact-match accuracy, hallucination rate, average chain length at convergence
baselines: standard CoT, self-consistency CoT with majority voting over 40 samples
expected_outcome: Formal proofs of T1-T3 constituting the first information-theoretic framework for CoT error propagation. Empirical results showing 8-12% accuracy gain on GSM8K over standard CoT, 30-40% hallucination reduction on StrategyQA vs. self-consistency, and a published open-source implementation of LCS-guided decoding compatible with any model exposing token log-probabilities.
title: Formal Consistency Bounds for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning via Logical Contraction Metrics

topic: large language model reasoning and planning
motivation: Current benchmarks like GSM8K and MATH measure final-answer accuracy but cannot distinguish models that arrive at correct answers via spurious pattern matching from those performing genuine multi-step logical reasoning. A model may score 80% on GSM8K while being internally inconsistent—reaching the same answer via contradictory intermediate steps when premises are slightly perturbed. This gap undermines trust in deployed reasoning systems and obscures where reasoning actually breaks down.
hypothesis: Models with higher raw accuracy on GSM8K will show a statistically significant accuracy-consistency gap (SCS at least 15 percentage points below raw accuracy, p<0.005 via McNemar's test), and premise-negation flip rates will be below 70% for all tested models, indicating that current LLMs rely partially on pattern matching rather than genuine logical reasoning. Specifically, we predict Llama-3-70B will show the largest accuracy-consistency gap (>20pp) while GPT-4o will show the smallest (<12pp).
method_sketch: 1. Construct a Premise Perturbation Test Suite (PPTS) from three existing benchmarks: GSM8K (arithmetic reasoning), ProofWriter (logical deduction), and StrategyQA (multi-hop factual reasoning). For each original problem, generate three variants: (a) a semantically equivalent restatement, (b) a single-premise negation that flips one key fact making the correct answer change, and (c) an irrelevant distractor insertion. 2. Evaluate five LLMs: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3-70B, Mistral-Large, and Gemini 1.5 Pro. Use chain-of-thought prompting (8-shot for GSM8K, 5-shot for ProofWriter, 5-shot for StrategyQA) with temperature=0 for reproducibility. 3. Define a Step-Consistency Score (SCS): for each problem cluster (original + 3 variants), extract intermediate reasoning steps using a structured output format, then compute (a) answer consistency rate across semantically equivalent pairs, (b) answer flip rate on premise-negation variants (should be ~100% for genuine reasoners), and (c) answer stability rate on distractor variants (should be ~100%). SCS = 0.4 * answer_flip_rate + 0.3 * answer_stability_rate + 0.3 * semantic_consistency_rate. 4. Baseline comparison: compare SCS against raw accuracy on original benchmarks to measure the accuracy-consistency gap. 5. Statistical analysis: use McNemar's test for pairwise model comparisons on binary consistency outcomes, Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (alpha=0.05/10=0.005), and compute Cohen's kappa for inter-rater reliability on step extraction (two annotators, 200-problem sample). Generate 500 PPTS problems per benchmark (1500 total, 6000 including variants) using GPT-4o with human validation of 20% sample.
metrics: Step-Consistency Score (SCS), raw accuracy, answer consistency rate, answer flip rate, answer stability rate, Cohen's kappa
baselines: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3-70B, Mistral-Large, Gemini 1.5 Pro
expected_outcome: Primary: A ranked leaderboard of 5 LLMs on SCS vs. raw accuracy, quantifying the accuracy-consistency gap for each. Secondary: Identification of which reasoning step positions (early vs. late in chain) show highest inconsistency rates. Tertiary: A publicly released PPTS dataset of 6000 problems enabling future benchmark comparisons. Quantitative targets: demonstrate that SCS correlates with raw accuracy at r<0.7 (Pearson), confirming it measures a distinct capability; show at least 3 of 5 models have premise-negation flip rates below 75% on ProofWriter.
title: Step-Consistency Probing: Measuring Logical Coherence Across Reasoning Chains in LLMs via Premise Perturbation

topic: large language model reasoning and planning
motivation: LLMs exhibit systematic goal drift and constraint forgetting beyond 15 steps on planning benchmarks. Prior retrieval-augmented approaches use semantic or recency-weighted similarity, which conflates surface proximity with causal relevance—a state causally enabling a distant future step is penalized while a causally irrelevant recent state is rewarded. Three distinct failure modes (attention dilution, positional encoding limits, absent explicit state tracking) remain conflated in the literature. No prior work grounds retrieval in a verified state-transition graph derived from the same formal model used for training, nor pre-registers a mediation analysis linking retrieval precision to coherence improvement.
hypothesis: HSA-Finetune+GraphRetrieval trained on the mixed corpus will achieve ≥15% higher goal achievement rate than the vanilla baseline on 20+ step problems in ALFWorld and LongPlan-30 (p < 0.01, Cohen's d ≥ 0.5), with ≤50% constraint violations relative to baseline. The mixed-corpus model will outperform the PDDL-only model by ≥8% on ALFWorld goal achievement. Graph-distance retrieval will yield ≥10% higher retrieval precision (fraction of retrieved states on shortest causal path) than cosine-only retrieval, and a pre-registered bootstrapped Sobel mediation test will show this precision gain mediates ≥50% of the coherence improvement over HSA-Finetune alone (95% CI excludes zero).
method_sketch: Four-condition within-subjects experiment across three benchmarks: ALFWorld, BigBench-Hard planning subtasks, and LongPlan-30 (1,200-problem PDDL-derived suite, 20–30 step solutions). LongPlan-30 construction: generate problems via FastDownward on Logistics/Blocksworld/Depots domains; retain only problems where (a) FastDownward with LMCUT succeeds (confirming well-formedness) and (b) zero-shot GPT-4o fails on ≥3 of 5 runs (confirming LLM difficulty), yielding a validated capability-gap subset. Report classical-planner success rate, human-solvability rate (sampled 50 problems, 3 annotators), and zero-shot GPT-4o failure rate as benchmark metadata. Conditions: (1) Baseline: GPT-4o chain-of-thought; (2) HSA-Prompt: structured world-state summary injected every K=5 steps; (3) HSA-Finetune: LoRA fine-tuning (rank=16, alpha=32) of Llama-3-70B on mixed corpus of PDDL Logistics/Blocksworld synthetic traces + ScienceWorld human-annotated trajectories (≥15 steps, augmented via PDDL solver on ScienceWorld state graph), with explicit state-delta annotations; (4) HSA-Finetune+GraphRetrieval: condition 3 augmented with graph-distance causal retrieval. Graph-distance retrieval: during fine-tuning, extract a state-transition graph G from PDDL traces where nodes are grounded states and edges are verified operator applications. At inference, embed the current state as a node query, identify its nearest node in G via cosine similarity, then score each candidate retrieved state s_i as score(s_i) = −d_G(current_node, node(s_i)), where d_G is shortest-path length in G (BFS over operator edges). States structurally upstream or downstream in the causal chain score highest regardless of temporal index, directly addressing the recency-conflation flaw. For ALFWorld (no PDDL ground truth), use ScienceWorld's underlying state graph as a proxy transition model. Domain-transfer ablation: train on PDDL-only vs. mixed corpus, evaluate on ALFWorld. Pre-registered mediation analysis (Baron-Kenny + bootstrapped Sobel test, 10,000 resamples): mediator = retrieval precision per episode (fraction of retrieved states on shortest causal path to goal, human-annotated on 150-episode held-out set), outcome = step-level coherence score, indirect effect reported with 95% CI. Evaluation: goal achievement rate, constraint violation count, step-level coherence scored by two independent judges—Llama-3-8B fine-tuned on stratified human annotations (500 examples, 80/20 split, calibration curve reported) and Claude-3-Opus; inter-judge κ reported per horizon-length stratum. Statistical analysis: mixed-effects ANOVA, Bonferroni-corrected pairwise comparisons, n=500 instances per condition per benchmark.
dataset: ALFWorld, BigBench-Hard, PDDL Logistics/Blocksworld/Depots, ScienceWorld trajectories
metrics: goal achievement rate, constraint violation count, step-level coherence score, retrieval precision per episode, inter-judge κ
expected_outcome: Primary: HSA-Finetune+GraphRetrieval achieves 15–25% absolute improvement in goal achievement on 20+ step horizons vs. baseline. Secondary: Mixed-corpus training closes ≥8% of ALFWorld transfer gap vs. PDDL-only. Graph-distance retrieval achieves ≥10% higher retrieval precision than cosine-only. Pre-registered Sobel test confirms retrieval precision mediates ≥50% of coherence improvement (95% CI excludes zero). Inter-judge κ ≥ 0.75 per stratum. Deliverables: LongPlan-30 benchmark (with metadata: classical-planner rates, LLM failure rates) released publicly; fine-tuned Llama-3-8B coherence judge released; full ablation and mediation analysis results.
title: Graph-Distance Causal Retrieval with Transition-Model Grounding for Long-Horizon Planning Coherence in LLMs
